ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Pad Thai. Nearest parent is a stir-fried rice noodle dish. A new FoodOn concept is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite noodle dishes — stir-fried rice noodles with seafood and egg (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Pad Thai; A03SM used as closest defensible parent for composite stir-fried noodle dishes with seafood.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Pad Thai as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Pad Thai with shrimp and bean sprouts weighs approximately 350 g, as served at Thai restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pad Thai — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table; HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Food Code F05060073)
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values drawn from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Food Code F05060073 (closest match). Composite dish values represent a standard Pad Thai preparation with shrimp, egg, bean sprouts, tofu, and peanuts. Minor adaptation applied to align with the specific shrimp-and-bean-sprout variant described.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — rice noodles, cooked; composite GI estimated for Pad Thai with tamarind sauce and palm sugar
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 47 derived from University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles (GI ~53 for plain rice noodles). Composite GI adjusted downward to approximately 47 to account for the acidic tamarind sauce (acidity reduces gastric emptying rate and lowers GI), protein from shrimp and egg, and fat from peanuts and oil — all of which attenuate glycaemic response. Glycaemic load of 30.9 calculated as (47 × 18.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 350g serving) / 100 = 30.9 g. Assessment confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 478 mg per 100 g, equating to approximately 1673 mg per standard 350 g serving (approximately 73% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit), driven primarily by fish sauce, dried shrimp, and soy seasoning.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Pad Thai to occasional consumption and request reduced fish sauce during preparation; the fish sauce is the primary sodium source and can be moderated without significantly altering the dish's flavour profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load — GI approximately 47 (low) for rice noodles, but a 350 g serving delivers approximately 65.8 g carbohydrate and a glycaemic load of approximately 31, which is moderate; added palm sugar contributes approximately 4.5 g sugar per 100 g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Pad Thai in controlled portions (approximately 200–250 g) and should request reduced palm sugar in the sauce to lower the glycaemic load per meal; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables further attenuates the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (478 mg/100 g) raises blood pressure risk; saturated fat is relatively low at 1.0 g/100 g; shrimp contributes cholesterol (68 mg/100 g) and omega-3 fatty acids; peanuts provide unsaturated fats and vitamin E.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and request reduced fish sauce to limit sodium intake; the dish's low saturated fat content and shrimp-derived omega-3 fatty acids are favourable, but the high sodium load per serving is the primary cardiovascular concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Shrimp provides omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory properties; tamarind has antioxidant properties; roasted peanuts add vitamin E and healthy unsaturated fats.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pad Thai is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the omega-3 content from shrimp and anti-inflammatory properties of tamarind may offer modest benefit, though sodium should still be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pad Thai with Shrimp and Bean Sprouts is Thailand's most internationally recognised noodle dish and a staple at Thai restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore. Thin, flat rice noodles (sen lek) are stir-fried in a screaming-hot wok with fresh shrimp, scrambled egg, crispy fried tofu, and crunchy bean sprouts, seasoned with a carefully balanced sauce of tamarind paste (sour), fish sauce (umami-salty), and palm sugar (sweet). The dish is garnished with crushed roasted peanuts, dried shrimp, fresh spring onion, and a lime wedge served on the side. The texture is a contrast of soft noodles, tender shrimp, and crunchy bean sprouts and peanuts. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g plate provides approximately 511 kcal, 26 g protein, 19 g fat, and 66 g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at approximately 1673 mg per serving, making it a concern for hypertension patients. The GI is approximately 47 (low), but the glycaemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 31, requiring portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Saturated fat is low at 1.0 g/100 g. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free. Originally promoted as a national dish by the Thai government in the 1930s to encourage rice noodle consumption, Pad Thai is now ubiquitous globally and is one of the most searched Thai dishes for nutritional information.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Food Code F05060073, both approved primary sources for Southeast Asian composite dishes. GI derived from University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous food (cooked rice noodles) with documented composite adjustment. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are populated from verified sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Pad Thai preparation is not separately reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table or HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the cited sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this preparation. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted for the composite dish composition (tamarind acidity, protein from shrimp and egg, fat from peanuts). Glycaemic load of 30.9 calculated per 350 g serving: (47 × 18.8 × 350) / 10000 = 30.9. Halal status marked false as fish sauce and dried shrimp sourcing cannot be confirmed halal without product-level verification. The ethnic_tags field in the input contained 'western' which appears to be a data entry error for a Thai dish; this has been corrected to Thai cuisine classification. Hindi local name not available in approved sources for this Thai dish; field set to null as Hindi is not a mandatory language for Thai cuisine under Project Veritas v2 rules.
